section: chapter
sentence: Large language model (LLM) serving has matured into a systems discipline where the dominant cost is often not FLOPs but how weights, activations, and the key--value (KV) cache move through memory hierarchies and how many discrete GPU kernels the host must launch per token.
title: Mind the Memory Gap: Unveiling GPU Bottlenecks in Large-Batch LLM Inference

section: chapter
sentence: In production, most teams hit the same contradiction: prefill benchmarks look excellent on datasheets, yet interactive decode still misses p99 latency SLOs after months of kernel tuning .
title: ELLIE: Energy-Efficient LLM Inference at the Edge Via Prefill-Decode Splitting

section: chapter
sentence: The durable misconception is that buying faster tensor cores or enabling FlashAttention ``fixes'' decode---when measured kernels-per-token and bytes-per-token often barely move because the stack remains operator-fragmented and host-orchestrated .
title: TPLA: Tensor Parallel Latent Attention for Efficient Disaggregated Prefill & Decode Inference

section: chapter
sentence: This chapter establishes the performance vocabulary used throughout the book: we separate from , quantify where latency hides in mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face stacks, and explain why cross-hardware compilation is not an optional portability exercise but a prerequisite for industrial decode optimization .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Prefill and Decode: Two Performance Regimes
sentence: In prefill, the model processes the entire prompt (or a prompt chunk) in parallel across sequence positions .
title: PDAcc: A 541 Tokens/s@Llama-1.2B Effective Throughput Prefill-Decode Disaggregated MCM-based Accelerator for Multi-task Large Language Model Applications

section: Prefill and Decode: Two Performance Regimes
sentence: Matrix multiplications are large, arithmetic intensity is relatively high, and modern accelerators can approach their peak tensor throughput .
title: PDAcc: A 541 Tokens/s@Llama-1.2B Effective Throughput Prefill-Decode Disaggregated MCM-based Accelerator for Multi-task Large Language Model Applications

section: Prefill and Decode: Two Performance Regimes
sentence: In decode, the model generates one new token per forward pass (per request, in the common batch-1 interactive setting), conditioned on all prior tokens through the KV cache .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Prefill and Decode: Two Performance Regimes
sentence: Prefill is frequently compute-bound on high-end GPUs: the GEMMs and attention blocks are wide enough to amortize memory latency .
title: TPLA: Tensor Parallel Latent Attention for Efficient Disaggregated Prefill & Decode Inference

section: Prefill and Decode: Two Performance Regimes
sentence: Decode, by contrast, repeatedly reads the full parameter set and growing KV tensors while performing only a thin slice of matmul work per token .
title: PDAcc: A 541 Tokens/s@Llama-1.2B Effective Throughput Prefill-Decode Disaggregated MCM-based Accelerator for Multi-task Large Language Model Applications

section: Prefill and Decode: Two Performance Regimes

title: TPLA: Tensor Parallel Latent Attention for Efficient Disaggregated Prefill & Decode Inference

sentence: Interactive assistants, coding copilots, and edge voice interfaces share a harsh constraint: users measure quality in time-to-next-token, often at batch size one .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: The Decode Pain Point: Single-Token, Low-Batch Latency
sentence: At batch-1 decode, each layer performs narrow GEMMs (e.g., a single new query vector against frozen weights) and attention that must touch the entire cached history .

section: The Decode Pain Point: Single-Token, Low-Batch Latency
sentence: The working set per token is small, but the read footprint is enormous: every weight matrix and every cached key/value vector must be visited again .

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: A na\"ive Transformers loop---one Python forward per token, eager PyTorch ops, separate kernels for layer norm, QKV projection, attention, MLP, residuals---mirrors the mathematical graph but not the memory hierarchy.
title: Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: Each operator materializes intermediates to global memory (GPU HBM or CPU DRAM), so a single decoder layer can trigger many read/write passes over tensors that could have stayed on chip given a different schedule .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: A decoder block decomposes into 10--20+ discrete kernels in unfused stacks .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: Python dispatch, CUDA driver launch, and framework bookkeeping run on the CPU while the accelerator idles between micro-kernels .
title: YiRage: Multi-Backend LLM Inference Optimization

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: Without paged or fused cache layouts, attention reads scatter through memory, wasting bandwidth on indexing and padding .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: Serving frameworks such as vLLM address capacity and KV fragmentation via PagedAttention and continuous batching , but they do not by themselves remove per-layer operator boundaries inside the forward pass.
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: FlashAttention reduces HBM traffic during prefill by IO-aware tiling ; Flash-Decoding adds a parallelization dimension along KV sequence length so batch-1 decode can still saturate SMs when context is long (reported up to end-to-end speedup at very long sequences) .
title: TPLA: Tensor Parallel Latent Attention for Efficient Disaggregated Prefill & Decode Inference

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: These are necessary optimizations, yet a full decoder step still contains MLPs, norms, rotary embeddings, and sampling logic that remain separable kernels unless a or compiler-generated fused schedule reunifies them .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: On CPUs the same fragmentation shows up as LLC misses rather than HBM stalls: each unfused boundary evicts cache lines that a fused decoder block could have kept resident across norm--matmul--activation chains .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Limits of Mainstream PyTorch and Hugging Face Inference
sentence: On XDNA-class NPUs, per-op dispatch is worse than wasteful---it violates static dataflow contracts and forces fallbacks to scalar host loops .
title: AMD XDNA Architecture

section: Quantifying Overheads: Launches, Memory, and Scheduling
sentence: For MoE models, decode latency can grow to multiple seconds per token at batch-1 not because GEMMs are huge, but because dispatch dominates: thousands of kernels per token with a per-launch floor on the order of microseconds yields tens of milliseconds of pure launch-path cost before framework overhead .

section: Quantifying Overheads: Launches, Memory, and Scheduling

section: Memory Bandwidth, Not Peak FLOPs

section: Multi-Hardware Pain Points: GPU, CPU, and NPU
sentence: A common pitfall is porting GPU-oriented fusion heuristics verbatim: medium-grain CUDA fusion that helps H100 can inflate CPU decode latency when it destroys cache blocking or forces cross-socket KV traffic on dual-socket servers .

section: Multi-Hardware Pain Points: GPU, CPU, and NPU
sentence: For CPU decode, the first lever is often KV tensor layout (contiguous head dimensions, page size aligned to cache lines) and coarse layer fusion that keeps QKV+MLP fragments in L3 for the duration of a token step---not micro-kernels tuned for warp occupancy .

section: Multi-Hardware Pain Points: GPU, CPU, and NPU
sentence: Spatial dataflow arrays (AMD XDNA) emphasize static tile assignment, on-chip buffer residency, and DMA pipelines with minimal dynamic scheduling .
title: AMD XDNA Architecture

section: Multi-Hardware Pain Points: GPU, CPU, and NPU
sentence: Decode favors fully static graphs, large fused regions, and pre-planned memory reuse; dynamic shapes and per-op Python dispatch are architectural mismatches .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Multi-Hardware Pain Points: GPU, CPU, and NPU
sentence: Where CUDA permits flexible grid launches and runtime shape guards, AIE tiles require fixed ADF graphs: if the compiler cannot prove tensor lifetimes fit on-chip SRAM, the schedule fails bring-up rather than degrading gracefully to DRAM-heavy eager paths .
title: CUDA Graphs

section: Multi-Hardware Pain Points: GPU, CPU, and NPU
sentence: That asymmetry is why NPU decode optimization ranks static fusion and DMA ping-pong above attention-kernel swaps---the binding constraint is residency proof, not FLOP count .
title: TPLA: Tensor Parallel Latent Attention for Efficient Disaggregated Prefill & Decode Inference

sentence: The roofline model plots attainable FLOP/s as , where arithmetic intensity (AI) is FLOPs per byte transferred from DRAM .

section: Roofline Intuition for Decoder Layers
sentence: For a decoder layer at batch-1, AI is roughly \[ AI 2N_{ {params} + 2N_ {KV}}N_{ {params} + N_ {KV}} \] in the crudest weight+KV read model (ignoring activations and writebacks), where grows linearly with context length .

section: Why Disaggregated Serving Changes the Optimization Target
sentence: When prefill and decode run on separate fleets, decode nodes see a steadier stream of single-token steps with hot weights resident on device .

section: Why Disaggregated Serving Changes the Optimization Target
sentence: That shifts engineering effort toward (i)~per-step latency, (ii)~KV cache locality, and (iii)~kernel persistence (CUDA Graphs, MegaKernels) rather than maximizing prompt throughput .

section: Why Disaggregated Serving Changes the Optimization Target
sentence: Disaggregation also exposes network and serialization costs if KV tensors move between pools---another reason compile-time dataflow planning must account for where tensors live across steps .

section: Tensor Cores and Decode: A Common Misconfiguration
sentence: Decode batch-1 often reduces effective to one (or a tiny batch), shrinking MMA utilization and increasing setup overhead .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Tensor Cores and Decode: A Common Misconfiguration
sentence: Hopper's Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) shifts address-generation and bulk copy work off scalar threads, but TMA still favors transfers large enough to amortize descriptor setup---small KV slices may remain latency-bound on regular loads .
title: Memory-Bound but Not Bandwidth-Limited: The Physical AI Inference Gap in Batch-1 LLM Decode

section: Tensor Cores and Decode: A Common Misconfiguration
sentence: The book's MegaKernel strategy therefore treats tensor cores as optional epilogues inside a dataflow schedule that first minimizes bytes and synchronization, then maps surviving GEMMs to MMA when dimensions justify it .

sentence: Prefill and decode obey different limits; interactive decode at low batch is dominated by memory traffic and orchestration overhead, not peak tensor FLOPs .

section: Chapter Summary
sentence: GPUs, CPUs, and NPUs each demand different fusion granularities and compilation strategies---there is no portable ``best kernel,'' only hardware-shaped schedules.
title: Mind the Memory Gap: Unveiling GPU Bottlenecks in Large-Batch LLM Inference

section: Chapter Summary
sentence: Industry kernels (FlashAttention, PagedAttention) and serving systems (vLLM) are necessary but insufficient for the extreme fusion pursued in this book .
